title: Wood finishing oils compared
subtitle: Linseed, tung, Danish, and teak oil: when to use which
excerpt: A guide to the four most common penetrating oil finishes used in wood-natural-craft: raw linseed, boiled linseed, tung oil, and Danish oil. Covers drying time, food safety, durability, and the right choice for spoons, boards, outdoor tools, and decorative pieces.
sourceNotes: Victorian cabinetry and household manuals for oil finishing methods; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for wood preservation and finishing.

glossaryTerms.definition: An oil that sets hard through oxidation rather than evaporation. Linseed and tung are drying oils; the hardened film is durable and water-resistant. Non-drying oils (vegetable cooking oils) do not set hard and will turn rancid in the wood over time.

A proprietary blend of drying oil (usually tung or linseed) and varnish, thinned with mineral spirits. Faster-drying than pure oil and producing a slightly tougher surface film. Not food-safe until fully cured.

A finish suitable for surfaces that will contact food once fully cured. Tung oil and raw linseed oil are food-safe when cured. Boiled linseed oil contains metallic driers that are not food-safe; Danish oil is not food-safe.

content.text: Penetrating oil finishes soak into the wood rather than sitting on the surface. They enhance the natural figure of the wood, require no specialist equipment to apply, and are easily maintained by re-oiling. The trade-off is a less durable surface than varnish or lacquer, and a longer wait between coats. The key question for each project is whether the finish must be
food-safe
once cured.
Raw linseed oil

Raw linseed is a slow-drying oil: each coat takes 2 to 3 days to set in a warm room. It is food-safe when cured and is the traditional finish for spoons, bowls, and chopping boards in the English craft tradition. Apply thin coats; a thick coat of raw linseed will stay tacky for weeks. The warm amber tone it produces deepens the colour of most hardwoods.
Boiled linseed oil

Boiled linseed (BLO) dries in 24 hours or less because it contains metallic siccative additives. It is faster to apply and produces a slightly harder surface than raw linseed. It is not food-safe and should not be used on items that will contact food. BLO is the standard finish for tool handles, garden tool shafts, and decorative carved pieces. It also prevents rust on the exposed metal of tools when applied to the metal as well as the wood.
Tung oil

Pure tung oil is the closest alternative to raw linseed: slower-drying (3 to 4 days per coat), food-safe when cured, and producing a slightly harder, more water-resistant film than raw linseed. It is the right choice for items exposed to intermittent water: bread boards, salad bowls, and outdoor-use wooden tools. Note that products labelled "tung oil finish" are often
Danish oil
blends, not pure tung oil.
Danish oil
Danish oil is a blend of
drying oil

and varnish, thinned for penetration. It dries in 4 to 8 hours per coat, builds a slightly shinier surface than pure oil, and is the fastest of the four to apply and maintain. It is not food-safe. Danish oil is the right choice for decorative turned pieces, frames, and furniture where appearance and maintenance ease matter more than food safety.
Quick reference
Spoons, bowls, bread boards in contact with food: raw linseed or pure tung oil.
Tool handles and garden implements: boiled linseed oil.
Decorative carved and turned pieces: Danish oil or boiled linseed.
Items exposed to weather: two coats of tung oil or a teak oil formulated for outdoor use.
